cards.back: Smallest deployable unit — one or more containers sharing network namespace and storage volumes
Manages ReplicaSets, provides declarative updates, rolling updates, and rollbacks for Pods
Ensures a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running at all times
Virtual cluster for resource isolation, RBAC scoping, and resource quotas within a cluster
Manages stateful apps with stable network identities, ordered deployment, and persistent storage per Pod
Ensures a Pod runs on every (or selected) node — used for logging agents, monitoring, CNI plugins
Creates one or more Pods that run to completion — used for batch processing
Runs Jobs on a cron schedule — e.g. backups, report generation
Stores non-confidential config as key-value pairs — mounted as env vars or volumes
Stores sensitive data (passwords, tokens, TLS certs) — base64-encoded, can be encrypted at rest
API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager — manages cluster state and scheduling decisions
Distributed key-value store that holds all cluster state — the single source of truth for K8s
Assigns Pods to nodes based on resource requirements, affinity, taints, and constraints
Runs controllers (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Job) that reconcile desired vs actual state
Agent on each node that ensures containers are running in Pods as specified by PodSpecs
Network proxy on each node that maintains iptables/IPVS rules for Service routing
CLI tool to interact with the K8s API server — create, inspect, update, delete resources
Software that runs containers — containerd (default in EKS), CRI-O. Docker was removed in K8s 1.24
Declarative: kubectl apply -f (desired state). Imperative: kubectl create/run (direct commands). Declarative is preferred
Key-value pair on objects used for selection and grouping — e.g. app=web, env=prod
Stable network endpoint (virtual IP) that load-balances traffic to a set of Pods matched by selector
Default type — internal-only virtual IP reachable within the cluster
Exposes Service on a static port (30000-32767) on every node's IP
Provisions an external load balancer (e.g. AWS NLB/CLB) that routes to NodePorts
HTTP/HTTPS routing rules — host/path-based routing to Services. Requires an Ingress Controller
Pod that implements Ingress rules — e.g. AWS ALB Ingress Controller, Nginx, Traefik
Container Network Interface — provides Pod networking. EKS uses Amazon VPC CNI (Pods get real VPC IPs)
Firewall rules for Pods — controls ingress/egress traffic by label, namespace, or CIDR
Cluster DNS server — resolves Service names to ClusterIPs (e.g. my-svc.my-ns.svc.cluster.local)
Service with clusterIP: None — returns Pod IPs directly via DNS, used with StatefulSets
Controller that creates DNS records (Route 53, etc.) automatically from Ingress/Service annotations
Every Pod gets its own IP. All Pods can communicate without NAT. Flat network enforced by CNI
Preserves client source IP by routing only to Pods on the receiving node — avoids extra hop
Next-gen replacement for Ingress — more expressive routing with Gateway, HTTPRoute, GRPCRoute resources
Cluster-level storage resource — provisioned statically by admin or dynamically via StorageClass
Request for storage by a Pod — binds to a PV that satisfies size and access mode requirements
Defines how PVs are dynamically provisioned — specifies provisioner, parameters, reclaim policy
ReadWriteOnce (single node), ReadOnlyMany (many nodes read), ReadWriteMany (many nodes read/write)
Retain (keep PV after PVC deleted), Delete (remove PV and backing storage), Recycle (deprecated)

cards.front: What is the EBS CSI driver?
cards.back: EKS add-on that provisions EBS volumes as PVs — supports gp3, io2, snapshots, encryption

cards.front: What is the EFS CSI driver?
cards.back: Provisions EFS file systems as PVs — supports ReadWriteMany, shared across Pods and nodes
Temporary volume created with the Pod, deleted when Pod is removed — useful for scratch space or sharing between containers
Specifies where a volume is mounted inside a container — path, readOnly, subPath
Role-Based Access Control — Role/ClusterRole define permissions, RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding assign them to users/groups/SAs
Role is namespace-scoped. ClusterRole is cluster-wide — also used for non-namespaced resources (nodes, PVs)
Identity for Pods to authenticate with the API server — each namespace has a default SA
Pod/container-level security settings — runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, capabilities, seccomp
Built-in admission controller (replaced PodSecurityPolicy) — enforces Privileged, Baseline, or Restricted profiles per namespace
Policy engine for K8s — enforces custom policies (e.g. no latest tag, require labels) via admission webhooks
IAM Roles for Service Accounts — lets EKS Pods assume IAM roles via OIDC without node-level credentials
Newer alternative to IRSA — simpler setup, no OIDC provider needed, uses EKS Pod Identity Agent add-on
EKS supports envelope encryption with KMS. Enable via encryptionConfig on cluster creation

cards.front: What is Secrets Store CSI Driver?
cards.back: Mounts secrets from external stores (AWS Secrets Manager, Parameter Store) as volumes in Pods
Intercepts API requests before persistence — Validating (reject bad requests) or Mutating (modify requests)
Linux capabilities removed from container — drop ALL then add only what's needed (e.g. NET_BIND_SERVICE)
Restricts syscalls a container can make — RuntimeDefault profile blocks dangerous syscalls
Prevents auto-mounting the SA token in Pods — reduces attack surface if Pod doesn't need API access
Managed Kubernetes service — AWS runs the control plane (API server, etcd), you manage worker nodes
AWS manages EC2 instances for you — auto-provisions, patches OS, handles draining during updates
You manage EC2 instances yourself — full control over AMI, instance type, bootstrap. More operational burden
Serverless compute for Pods — no nodes to manage. AWS provisions right-sized VMs per Pod. Uses Fargate Profiles
Defines which Pods run on Fargate — matches by namespace and optional labels

cards.front: What is the VPC CNI plugin?
cards.back: EKS default CNI — assigns real VPC IP addresses to Pods. Pods are directly routable in the VPC
Assigns /28 prefixes instead of individual IPs to ENIs — increases Pod density per node (110+ Pods)

cards.front: What is the AWS Load Balancer Controller?
cards.back: Provisions ALBs for Ingress and NLBs for LoadBalancer Services — replaces legacy in-tree cloud provider
AWS-managed operational software — VPC CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, EBS CSI driver. Updated via EKS API
Public (API accessible from internet), Private (VPC only), or Public+Private (public with private routing for nodes)
IAM authenticates (aws-iam-authenticator), K8s RBAC authorizes. aws-auth ConfigMap or EKS access entries map IAM to K8s identities
Newer API-based auth (replaces aws-auth ConfigMap) — maps IAM principals to K8s groups/permissions directly
Control plane upgraded first (in-place, managed by AWS), then node groups. One minor version at a time
Open-source node autoscaler — provisions right-sized EC2 instances directly (not ASG-based). Faster than Cluster Autoscaler
CA scales ASG node groups (predefined instance types). Karpenter provisions optimal instances per Pod requirements — more flexible, faster
CLI tool to create and manage EKS clusters — wraps CloudFormation. Good for quick setup, not for production IaC
$0.10/hour per cluster ($73/mo) for control plane + EC2/Fargate costs for worker nodes
Run EKS on your own infrastructure (on-prem, VMs) — same K8s distribution and tooling as cloud EKS
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler — scales Pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics
Vertical Pod Autoscaler — adjusts Pod CPU/memory requests based on actual usage. Can conflict with HPA
Minimum CPU/memory guaranteed to a container — used by scheduler for placement decisions
Maximum CPU/memory a container can use — CPU is throttled, memory over-limit causes OOMKill
Guaranteed (requests=limits), Burstable (requests<limits), BestEffort (no requests/limits). Eviction order: BestEffort first
Taints repel Pods from nodes. Tolerations on Pods allow scheduling on tainted nodes. Used for dedicated/GPU nodes
Rules that constrain which nodes a Pod can schedule on — based on node labels (required or preferred)
Schedule Pods together (affinity) or apart (anti-affinity) based on labels — e.g. spread across AZs
Limits voluntary disruptions — ensures minAvailable or maxUnavailable Pods during drains, upgrades, scaling
topologySpreadConstraints distribute Pods evenly across zones, nodes, or other topology domains
Sets default and max resource requests/limits per container in a namespace
Limits total resource consumption (CPU, memory, object count) per namespace
Checks if container is alive — restarts container on failure. HTTP GET, TCP socket, or exec command
Checks if container can serve traffic — removes Pod from Service endpoints on failure
Checks if container has started — disables liveness/readiness until success. For slow-starting apps
EKS monitoring — collects metrics (CPU, memory, network) and logs from nodes and Pods via CloudWatch agent
Managed Prometheus-compatible monitoring — scrapes K8s metrics, stores in AWS, query with PromQL

cards.front: What is Fluent Bit on EKS?
cards.back: Lightweight log forwarder — ships container logs to CloudWatch, S3, OpenSearch. Common EKS logging pattern
logs: container stdout/stderr. describe: events, conditions, resource details — use describe for scheduling/startup issues
Cluster add-on that collects resource metrics from kubelets — required for HPA and kubectl top

cards.front: How to enable Traefik access logs in k3s?
cards.back: Create a HelmChartConfig in /var/lib/rancher/k3s/server/manifests/ with logs.access.enabled: true

cards.front: How to view Traefik access logs?
cards.back: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=traefik -f

cards.front: What do Traefik access logs show?
cards.back: Client IP, timestamp, method, path, status code, response size, matched ingress route, backend Pod, and response time

cards.front: What is a HelmChartConfig in k3s?
cards.back: CRD that overrides values for k3s-managed Helm charts (Traefik, CoreDNS) — placed in the server manifests directory

cards.front: How to add Let's Encrypt TLS to Traefik in k3s?
cards.back: Add ACME cert resolver via additionalArguments in HelmChartConfig — set email, storage path, and httpChallenge entrypoint

cards.front: How to redirect HTTP to HTTPS in Traefik?
cards.back: Create a Middleware CRD with redirectScheme (scheme: https, permanent: true) and a separate Ingress for the web entrypoint that references it
HTTP status code that permanently redirects while preserving the request method (unlike 301 which may change POST to GET)
Insufficient resources, no matching nodes (taints/affinity), PVC not bound, or ResourceQuota exceeded. Check kubectl describe pod
Container starts and crashes repeatedly — check kubectl logs. Common: wrong command, missing config, OOMKilled, failing health check
Can't pull container image — wrong image name/tag, missing registry credentials, or network issue
Check selector matches Pod labels, Pod is Ready, endpoints exist (kubectl get endpoints). Check NetworkPolicy
kubelet not running, resource pressure (disk/memory/PID), or network issues. Check kubectl describe node and kubelet logs
Container exceeded memory limit — increase limits, fix memory leak, or tune JVM/runtime heap settings
Image architecture mismatch — e.g. arm64 image on amd64 node. Build with --platform linux/amd64

cards.front: How to debug a running Pod?
cards.back: kubectl exec -it pod -- sh. Or kubectl debug for ephemeral containers on distroless images

cards.front: How to check Pod resource usage?
cards.back: kubectl top pod (requires metrics-server). For historical data use Prometheus/CloudWatch
Package manager for K8s — charts are templated YAML bundles. Install, upgrade, rollback releases
Chart.yaml (metadata), values.yaml (defaults), templates/ (K8s manifests with Go templating)
Idempotent deploy — installs if release doesn't exist, upgrades if it does
GitOps controller — watches a Git repo and syncs K8s cluster state to match. Declarative, auditable deployments
GitOps toolkit by CNCF — reconciles cluster state from Git. Supports Helm, Kustomize, plain YAML
GitOps: cluster pulls desired state from Git (ArgoCD/Flux). Push: CI pipeline applies to cluster (kubectl/helm). GitOps is more auditable
Additional container in a Pod — handles logging, proxying, or config. K8s 1.28+ has native sidecar support
Runs before app containers start — used for setup tasks (DB migration, config download). Must complete successfully
Custom Resource Definition — extends the K8s API with your own resource types (e.g. Certificate, VirtualService)
Controller + CRDs that automate complex app lifecycle — e.g. database provisioning, backup, failover
Infrastructure layer for service-to-service communication — mTLS, traffic management, observability. Istio, Linkerd, App Mesh
Default Deployment strategy — replaces Pods gradually. maxSurge and maxUnavailable control the pace
Run two Deployments (blue/green), switch Service selector to the new version. Instant rollback by switching back
Route small % of traffic to new version — via weighted Ingress, Service Mesh, or Argo Rollouts
Template-free YAML customization — overlays for different environments. Built into kubectl (kubectl apply -k)
Metadata key that prevents resource deletion until a controller completes cleanup — e.g. PV data deletion
